speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four, interview with Micheline Marmor, and it's January 14th 1997. So we were talking about the-- the American troops being near-- near Paris, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Oh, yes, near Paris. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Paris. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when they were caught, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And-- and the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- but I had-- Yeah, I had nothing to do in Paris. I was-- all this time I was in Nice. And I remember, first of all, you know, like-- there was no light at the end of tunnel. Absolutely. I mean, I was wondering, will-- it will never end. Because in the-- you never dreamed that-- you know, that you'd be liberated. And I say, it won't last. We all are going to be caught in the end. So it was-- and then, you know, it was not all-- one day we decided we deserve vacation. So we all went to ski in Alpes-Maritimes, which is the mountain, you know, not too far from there. And we spent a week vacation there. We deserve it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you went on vacation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the whole group. And then I-- as I ski, I sprained ankle-- sprained my ankle, and then I had to stay a week. I couldn't move. I had to stay. And then, because I-- I just-- I sketch everybody. I had a sketch book. And I spent that week to sketch. I have a whole sketch book of these people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So once in a while, we are young. [LAUGHS] But there was a lot of friendship. And then, when I went to visit these people, they keep asking me, when is the war going to be over? Well, nobody knew. So I said, well, I think, August 20. I said August 20 because it was my birthday. And what do you think? The Ally came in Nice on August 20. [LAUGHS] So people told me, eh, you really knew, eh? [LAUGHS] I really didn't know, but that was wishful thinking. That was August 20th.

speaker: interviewee
text: August 20. Yeah, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: '45, yes, I think. [LAUGHS] You know, everything is more than 50 years ago. I had to think a lot to remember all this. First I thought, you know, I put it back in my mind, and I thought I forgot. And then when one story, one fight came, and another, and another, and then after, I remembered too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take food to the people in hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, oh, yes. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got money from-- not too much food, because we give them the paper to get food. But we got money from the United State, so we'll give them money if they didn't have any.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you, then, on August 20th, 1945?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in my apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you could-- hardly could go out. You know, like-- they will turn over a car and-- you know, people got really excited. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then it's-- it was unbelievable that it was over, and we are still alive. Unfortunately, a lot of our friend were not. It was luck, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of-- were the Germans still in Nice, in August 20th?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Well, they were all there. They were fighting, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the fighting in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't. No. I didn't go. I wanted to survive, you know, rather than to get killed the last-- I'm not going to get killed the last [LAUGHS] day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you see the-- then the Americans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I say is, I don't know how long I stay. But everybody wanted to go back to their family. So I couldn't write to-- to my parents or anything. It was very difficult. So we all decided to go back to Paris and meet there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Except-- so everybody, you know-- we kiss, and we say, everybody leave to their own family. So I finally went-- went back to Paris. But the sad part is, because we left, they give our apartment to a war widow. And the war widow, in French, you don't touch them. And we couldn't get back to our apartment for a long time, because we had to take a lawyer, and that was my parents' way of living. That was their life. So it was not that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the shop-- their-- their shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, that was-- after-- after we took a lawyer, and went to court and to court. And finally, we finally got the apartment. But the day-- [INAUDIBLE] the day we're supposed to-- to take the apartment, there was a lot of collaborators who didn't want us to go in. They were there to stop us, after the war. But that day we couldn't go. So this time we came with the league. You have the same thing here. It's an organization against these antisemitic-- they came. And then nobody was there, and we went in. And life started again. And my brother came from Switzerland. But-- no. We met at my mother's sister. And we all met there. And actually, we were lucky, because we all survived. And the closest person who didn't survive is my mother's sister. She was deported in Paris, and she-- we never knew what happened to her. She never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you change your name back to Kaplan then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had both name. When-- when I came in this country, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they asked me what name-- if I wanted another name. So I say, well I will take both name. I take Kaplan Beaumont, and my brother too. My-- my brother-in-law told us it will be a good idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did life go on the way it was before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: So where do you want to talk about, this country, or France?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris. [INAUDIBLE] Yeah, in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, but-- well, oh, yes, I went back to-- to the [INAUDIBLE], and this time I study very seriously. I took-- I studied in the [INAUDIBLE] with [? Sowerby-- ?] he's a painter which is quite known, like Picasso. And I studied fresco with Ducot de [? Lai. ?] This is the name of the artist. And this was the happiest time of my life, actually, I must say. Because I was free. I was doing what I liked to do. So that's what I did for-- till I came in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we came here because my-- my mother didn't want to stay in this country, in France. She said she has been so scared [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for us, you know, that she was scared that we'll be deported that she-- and she always-- she has always loved the United States. She always wanted to come here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we apply [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to go, but it took-- took a long time. But I went on the French quota, so I went before. My father was born in Russia [INAUDIBLE]. So then they had to go on the Russian quota. But it's not the same quota as us. So I came with my brother. My brother was younger. He came on my quota. So I came on the French quota, before my parents. Did they have the business there? Or did they start up the business again in-- in Paris, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They started a business in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I mean in Paris. Were they running the business after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in Paris. Yeah, they did have the business. But it was never the same, because, well, little by little, you know, you have to go back to-- and then they stole so many things. You know, we gave things to people to hold, and they never return it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your brother came to-- to America together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We came to Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '46, I think. Yeah. Around '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- Why Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my sister lived there. My sister lived there, and we came and-- and stay with her in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then later on-- four months later, I think-- my parents came in New York. Then I went to New York to live with my [INAUDIBLE]. They didn't do too well here because nobody, like-- the kind of business they have, you have to be in the east side. And nobody told them. They took a place in the west side. [INAUDIBLE] The way business work in this country is completely different. You have to advertise. In-- in France, it's by word of mouth. One customer bring another.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't-- they didn't know how to develop a business like they did in France. But they did well. Finally, they moved to Chicago, and they had a good business there. But I didn't want-- I didn't want to go back to Chicago. I wanted to stay. I stayed in New York. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got a job at the French embassy. First-- actually, this is why my English is not perfect. Because when I came here, instead of going to school and learn English, I was learning beautiful French at the Embassy, because you have to speak, you know, correctly. My work first was they had-- they had exhibition in the-- which is a cultural service, in-- in Fifth Avenue, which is now the consulate. They have exhibition about Victor Hugo, least known Baudelaire, you know, all these-- everything about French culture.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my work was to help the cultural attache, the man in charge, to put to exhibit. Like, there were these big vitrine, you know, where you exhibit-- I don't know. You call it? Yeah, vitrine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vitrine? Same one? So he used to give me the work, and then I will dispose it. I make it look-- and then, I will put the pictures the way, you know. It was a beautiful room-- big, four-- three windows in Fifth Avenue at 74th. And then I will arrange it. And then when I'm ready, the man from the Metropolitan-- the worker from the Metropolitan Museum will come, and then I will show them to hang. And then I was the receptionist. When people come, I will receive them. And that was my work for about five years. Then [INAUDIBLE] came, [LAUGHS] and wanted to save money. So no more-- no more exhibition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got a job through a friend of mine, working for Alva studio, which is the place where they make reproduction for the Metropolitan Museum, if you buy this sculpture and everything. Like, this sculpture down there, come from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my work was to make it look like bronze. Or if there was some Egyptian design, you know, that was the job I did for a little while. And then I got married. Where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I work at the French Embassy, I I decide-- I didn't want to study painting, because I-- I study enough in France. But I was interested to start sculpture. So I register at the Brooklyn Museum, when they had very good teacher then. I think my teacher was Hebald, Milton Hebald. And so I went there. Because I work, I went there at night. Then my husband was studying on the GI Bill. He was studying etching and-- and then he-- we met there, met at the Brooklyn Museum. People think it's funny. [LAUGHS] And then we got married. And then we-- my husband worked, and then I didn't work, actually. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I raised the two daughters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, my husband worked for a different company. But he-- when we moved-- he worked for [? Groman. ?] [INAUDIBLE] He was the technical illustrator. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we moved to the-- because-- the first company he work with was Liton, I think, and they moved to the Island. So they help people to move. That's how we came in Long Island. And I like Long Island. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any statement that-- that you would like to make before we

speaker: interviewee
text: Close?

speaker: interviewer
text: We close the tape? Is there anything that you'd like to-- like to say about your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, it's-- well, it's like two life, one life here, and a-- another life in-- in France. It's two life. But I-- I always wonder, what will ever happen if something like this happened in the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: You think it could?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it could, but what will be the reaction of the people if something like this could happen? And that was my-- and I don't know to answer. I don't think it will happen, something this. There is too much freedom here. Yeah, I was-- what puzzled me is, when I came in this country, nobody ask you for your paper. Never. Never once I have been asked for my paper. And in France, you can't even go to the bathroom without showing your paper. That's how strict they were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I want to-- to say is there was a lot of collaborator. But there were wonderful French people who did that, risked their life, to help, to-- like in the-- you know, in farm, in a lot of thing, private housing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's all. [LAUGHS] Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. I'm glad it's over. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, this is my mother and my father. And I think it was taken in 1920 something, maybe the end of 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris. As you see, my mother always liked nice-looking clothes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yeah, this picture is taken in Paris. And the three children, my sister on the right side, Elaine, my brother, Samson Claude, and me, Micheline. And I think it's a nice picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I look at it, I don't know. I must have been maybe four. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK. This was taken about 1932. And we had a country house close to Paris, which was called [? Liverde. ?] And this is my father's car. And on the right is my aunt, my mother's sister, which is the only person in the family who was deported and never came back. Then me, my brother, and my sister, and then my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a good time then, [LAUGHS] a good time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my first identity card. I was 16. And of course, it's in Paris. And this is-- well you see my name. And then, finally, this is when you had to go to the police station and have this big Juive, means Jew for a woman. And this was-- I was-- I called a kiss of death. Because with this paper, you don't survive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's about all I have to say about this. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my identity card during the underground. And this is the name who saved my life. I was no more Micheline Kaplan. Now I'm Micheline Beaumont. And I was born in Grenoble. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I kept this all through the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like I said, it saved my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Finally, at the end of the war, when the Ally came, we got a noti-- an identification paper to show that we belong to the French Resistance. And it was done in September, '44. And if you showed this, you could do anything you want with that card. I could arrest anybody. It was a very powerful card after the-- at the end the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever arrest anyone? Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] I didn't arrest anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is front of the card that we just saw, FFI, Forces Francaises de L'Interieur. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, this picture was taken in June, '41, to refer to the story where I didn't want to be ashamed to wear the Jewish star, and I-- I say that I was wearing bright yellow earrings. And-- and here it is. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1944, in the studio of the photographer where I will go and pick up my false paper. And if somebody-- if somebody was there and it was dangerous, I will just say, oh, I come for my picture. And this is the picture that I will-- that I will get. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Shortly-- shortly after the war ended, in Nice, in 1944, we were so glad to go back to this place, which is the house I was born and we lived all the time, except during the German occupation. And this is Cannes [? Revingnon. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this-- this picture is 25 years later, when I took a trip. And that's the picture, 25 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris. In 1954, magazine had an article called And they pick up seven artists, and I was one of them. And Gordon Park, which is a known photographer-- I was very young, at that time-- working for magazine, came to my apartment and took this picture with-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On top right is a drawing I did. The sculpture is my husband. And the wood sculpture is one of my sculptures. And the painting on the easel is my husband. [LAUGHS] This picture is taken in 1977. 1977 in-- we bought a house in Long Island, and that's where we are. This-- this is me on the right, my cat, Daisy, and my husband, Seymour. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is my two daughter. On the right is Nadine McLaron, and on the left is Rachelle Marmor.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken about 10 years ago, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 15 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my granddaughter, Jennifer Marie, which I love dearly. And she is two and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Recently, a few months ago, maybe not even a few months. Maybe a month ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here on Long Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Long Island, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
